title: Text Processing: grep, sed, awk
description: grep, sed, and awk are the three pillars of Linux text processing. At Enterprise, every engineer — from SRE to ML researcher — uses these tools daily. Mastering them signals that you think in Unix pipelines: finding patterns, transforming output, and extracting insights without needing to write a script.

sections.title: grep — Global Regular Expression Print
sections.description: grep searches files for lines matching a pattern. It's the first tool you reach for when investigating anything on a Linux system. Production engineers use grep constantly — finding error messages, filtering log output, checking configurations across directories.
sections.codeBlock:
# Basic usage
grep 'error' /var/log/syslog              # Find all lines containing 'error'
grep -i 'warning' app.log                 # Case-insensitive
grep -v 'debug' app.log                   # Invert match (exclude debug)
grep -c 'timeout' access.log              # Count matches
grep -n 'exception' stacktrace.log        # Show line numbers

# Useful flags
grep -r 'TODO' /src/                      # Recursive search in directory
grep -l 'config' /etc/**/*.conf           # List only filenames with matches
grep -w 'class' schema.sql               # Whole word match (not 'subclass')
grep -A3 -B2 'FATAL' app.log             # Show 2 lines before, 3 after context
grep -E 'error|fail|timeout' log          # Extended regex (multiple patterns)

# Piped grep — filter command output
dmesg | grep -i 'error'                   # Kernel errors
ps aux | grep 'python'                    # Find python processes (but grep itself matches!)
ps aux | grep '[p]ython'                  # Trick: brackets prevent grep from matching itself

# Production scenario: count unique error types in a 10GB log
grep -oP 'ERROR \K\w+' app.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
# -o: only matching text, -P: perl regex, \K: discard before
# (This extracts the word after 'ERROR ')

sections.title: sed — Stream Editor
sections.description: sed transforms text streams. It's grep's more powerful cousin — find patterns AND modify them. sed is essential for batch edits, text replacement, and pipeline formatting.
sections.codeBlock:
# Basic substitution (the most common operation)
sed 's/old/new/' file.txt                 # Replace first occurrence per line
sed 's/old/new/g' file.txt                # Replace ALL occurrences per line (global)
sed 's/old/new/gi' file.txt               # Global + case-insensitive
sed 's/old/new/2' file.txt                # Replace second occurrence only

# In-place editing (careful!)
sed -i 's/foo/bar/g' config.conf          # Edit file in place (Linux version)
sed -i '' 's/foo/bar/g' config.conf       # macOS/BSD: empty extension = no backup
sed -i.bak 's/foo/bar/g' config.conf      # Create backup with .bak extension

# Line addressing
sed '10s/old/new/' file.txt               # Replace on line 10 only
sed '5,10s/old/new/g' file.txt            # Replace on lines 5-10
sed '5,$s/old/new/g' file.txt             # Replace from line 5 to end

# Delete lines
sed '/debug/d' app.log                    # Delete lines matching 'debug'
sed '1,5d' file.txt                       # Delete first 5 lines
sed '/^$/d' file.txt                      # Delete empty lines

# Print specific lines
sed -n '10,20p' file.txt                  # Print lines 10-20 (like head/tail combo)
sed -n '/ERROR/p' /var/log/syslog         # Print only lines matching ERROR

# Multiple commands
sed -e 's/foo/bar/' -e 's/abc/xyz/' file.txt

# Production scenario: mask IP addresses in a log file
sed -E 's/[0-9]{1,3}(\.[0-9]{1,3}){3}/***.***.***.***/g' access.log

sections.title: awk — Pattern Scanning and Processing Language
sections.description: awk is a full programming language designed for text processing. It splits lines into fields, processes them with C-like syntax, and is unmatched for extracting columns, computing statistics, and reformatting output. If grep is a scalpel and sed is a Swiss Army knife, awk is a workshop.

sections.title: xargs — Build and Execute Commands
sections.description: xargs takes input from stdin and passes it as arguments to another command. It's the glue that connects find/grep output to commands like rm, chmod, or cp. Understanding xargs is what separates intermediate from advanced CLI users.
sections.codeBlock:
# Basic usage: pass filenames to a command
find /tmp -name '*.tmp' -type f | xargs rm -f    # Remove temp files
find . -name '*.py' | xargs wc -l                 # Count lines in all Python files

# Handle filenames with spaces (use -0 with null-separated)
find . -name '*.log' -print0 | xargs -0 rm

# Parallel execution
find . -name '*.gz' | xargs -P 4 gunzip          # Unzip 4 files at once

# Replace string placeholder
ls *.txt | xargs -I {} cp {} /backup/             # Copy each file to /backup

# Prompt before executing (safe mode)
find . -name '*.swp' | xargs -p rm               # 'y' to confirm each

# Production scenario: batch chmod with confirmation
find /app/deploy -type f | xargs -p -I {} chmod 644 {}

# Count lines across source files
grep -rl 'TODO' /src | xargs wc -l | tail -1     # Total TODO lines

sections.title: Pipeline-Fu: Combining Everything
sections.description: The real power comes from combining these tools. These compound pipelines are the kind of thing Production engineers type daily — and they make excellent interview problems.
sections.codeBlock:
# Find top 5 slowest requests from an nginx log
# ($NF is request time in seconds)
awk '{print $NF, $0}' access.log | sort -rn | head -5 | awk '{print $2}'

# Find all unique IPs accessing /api, count requests
awk '$7 ~ /^\/api/ {print $1}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# Report on HTTP status codes with percentage
awk '{
    total++
    status[$9]++
} END {
    print "Total requests:", total;
    for (s in status) printf "%s: %d (%.1f%%)\n", s, status[s], status[s]/total*100
}' access.log

# Find processes with high memory, format as CSV
ps aux --sort=-%mem | awk 'NR>1 {printf "%s,%s,%s\n", $2, $11, $4}' | head -10

# Monitor log file in real-time for error patterns
tail -f /var/log/app.log | grep --line-buffered 'ERROR' | awk '{print $1, $3, $NF}'

# Batch rename files with sed
for f in *.txt; do mv "$f" "$(echo "$f" | sed 's/ /_/g')"; done

# Production scenario: find files modified in last 24h, compress them
find /var/log/myapp -mtime -1 -name '*.log' | xargs tar -czf backup.tar.gz

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'Using a one-liner, find the top 10 IP addresses hitting your web server from an nginx access log.'

A: 'awk '{print $1}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10''

Q: 'You need to replace all occurrences of 'http://' with 'https://' in a directory of config files, but only in lines that contain 'api.' Do it without an editor.'

A: 'sed -i '/api/s|http://|https://|g' /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/*' — the /api/ pattern limits sed to lines containing 'api', and I use | as delimiter to avoid escaping slashes.'

Q: 'Parse /etc/passwd and list all users with login shells that aren't /sbin/nologin or /bin/false.'

A: 'awk -F: '$7 !~ /nologin|false/ {print $1, $7}' /etc/passwd''

sections.description:
• GNU Grep Manual
  • GNU Sed Manual
  • GAWK (Awk) Manual — The definitive reference
  • sed & awk — Dale Dougherty & Arnold Robbins (O'Reilly)

learningObjectives: Understand grep — Global Regular Expression Print
Understand awk — Pattern Scanning and Processing Language
Understand xargs — Build and Execute Commands

analogy.title: Text Processing as a Factory Line
analogy.description: grep is the quality inspector (filters lines). sed is the find-and-replace machine. awk is the programmable assembler that builds reports. sort organizes inventory. uniq removes duplicates. Pipes (|) are conveyor belts moving text between stations.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: grep — Global Regular Expression Print
practiceLabs.task: Run `grep` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man grep` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: sed — Stream Editor
practiceLabs.task: Run `dmesg` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man dmesg` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: awk — Pattern Scanning and Processing Language
practiceLabs.task: Run `ps` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man ps` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `grep` | grep — Global Regular Expression Print |
| `dmesg` | grep — Global Regular Expression Print |
| `ps` | grep — Global Regular Expression Print |
| `sed` | sed — Stream Editor |
| `awk` | awk — Pattern Scanning and Processing Language |
| `if` | awk — Pattern Scanning and Processing Language |
| `errors` | awk — Pattern Scanning and Processing Language |
| `find` | xargs — Build and Execute Commands |
| `ls` | xargs — Build and Execute Commands |
| `grep` | xargs — Build and Execute Commands |
| `awk` | Pipeline-Fu: Combining Everything |
| `total` | Pipeline-Fu: Combining Everything |
| `status` | Pipeline-Fu: Combining Everything |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man grep`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: text processing operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.symptom: Permission issues accessing text processing resources
troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is grep — Global Regular Expression Print?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The grep — Global Regular Expression Print is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `grep` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the text processing directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a text processing directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a text processing scenario, your monitoring alerts show grep failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  In my first week at Amazon, a principal engineer solved a production incident by typing a single awk pipeline that analyzed 50GB of logs in 10 seconds
FAANG scale, you can't always load data into Python or SQL — sometimes you need to find the needle in a haystack with nothing but the tools built into every Linux server since 1970
productionTips: production incident by typing a single awk pipeline that analyzed 50GB of logs in 10 seconds
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, text processing: grep, sed, awk is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
